survival_reason: The mutation changes the boundary condition from `row >= rows` to `row >= rows + 1`, which only affects execution when the block index equals `rows`. In typical kernel launches, the grid dimension is set to `rows` blocks (blockIdx.x ∈ [0, rows-1]), so no thread ever has `row == rows`. The stress tests kept the same input shape [128, 4000], which likely translates to launching exactly 128 blocks. Therefore, the mutant behaves identically to the original since the mutated condition is never evaluated with `row == rows`. The mutant survives because test coverage does not include kernel launches with extra (out-of-bounds) blocks.
kill_strategy: Kill the mutant by launching the kernel with grid dimension > rows (i.e., more blocks than rows). Specifically, configure the kernel launch to have at least `rows+1` blocks. This will cause block `row == rows` to execute in the mutant (leading to out-of-bounds memory access or incorrect computation) while the original would early-return. The input tensor shape can remain [128, 4000], but the kernel launch parameters must be changed to, e.g., grid=(129,1,1).
recommendations: Improve the test suite by varying kernel launch configurations, not just input tensor values/shapes. Specifically, test edge cases where grid dimension exceeds valid data bounds (testing boundary guards). Additionally, the kernel code could be made more robust by using assertion or explicit clamp on `row` index, but the real issue is insufficient launch configuration coverage in testing.

robustness_suggestion: The kernel lacks proper grid dimension validation. Currently, it assumes the grid size (number of blocks) exactly matches the problem size (rows). This creates a brittle coupling between launch configuration and kernel logic. The mutation survived because tests didn't trigger the boundary case. A robust kernel should validate both block and thread indices against problem dimensions independently of launch configuration. This prevents out-of-bounds memory access if grid dimensions are miscalculated or if the kernel is reused in different contexts.
